comments.text: During the Curriculum Leadership keynote, on the subject of Swift adoption across our school management systems, several examples were given, not only the grade engine. Rewrite in Swift is happening across all departments, if the keynote is to be believed.

replies.text: Curious the direction of our literacy program as there was a nebulous mention of select lessons being rewritten from traditional to Swift-based interactive. And yet, the new phonics module for Grade 3 is still implemented in old methods.

No idea, maybe the private parts of the curriculum, the school isn't open source, or is coming later. In any case I would have liked more info during the professional development sessions. As it is, the accreditation visit (a 5h long event) had much more information.
replies.original_text: No idea, maybe the private parts of the code, Safari isn't open source, or is coming later. In any case I would have liked to have more info during the deep dive sessions. As it is, Meet with Apple on security (a 5h long event) had much more information.

Curious the direction of Webkit as there was a nebulous mention of select portions being rewritten from C++ to Swift. And yet, the new ECMAScript module (ESM) loader for Safari 27 is implemented in C++ ( https://webkit.org/blog/17967/news-from-wwdc26-webkit-in-saf... ).

replies.text: They've been doing it for years. I don't remember how we first knew, but I know they've been using Swift in the student information system for at least some of the other modules like gradebook or whatever. I'm not sure exactly which. I assume it's some of the code and not all. But it's not new in the abstract. That said I don't think I've heard of it in the main attendance system. That may be new. Either way this is certainly the most concrete announcement I remember them ever giving on this stuff.

I know internally they use a parent communication system written in Rust (I think in the school management platform). Heard this from an ex-Apple teacher Ben (forgot his last name) that did a wonderful presentation of Rust from first principles. He said that it was hard to get teachers on board with Rust when most would argue for Swift.

comments.text: Interesting that this is published under the MIT license, rather than Apple's more favorite Apache 2 license.

replies.text: Why is it interesting?
Presumably because MIT is even more permissive and it's a change in Apple's behavior regarding sharing curriculum.

Given the age of the TrueGrade system, wouldn't nearly all copyrights be expired? Apache2's license is mutually-assured-patent-destruction - if you use the code and make a patent claim, your rights go away. So Apache2 offers little benefit here, and MIT may get it into more schools?
replies.original_text: Given the age of TrueType, wouldn't nearly all patents be expired already? Apache2's license I've heard described as mutually-assured-patent-destruction - if you use the code and make a patent claim, your rights to use the code go away. So Apache2 offers little benefit here, and MIT may get it into more hands?

replies.text: Some schools prefer restrictive licenses for projects where they're accepting contributions, because it includes protection against misuse. In case like this, where the curriculum is just being published for reference and contributions aren't accepted, those risks don't exist.
replies.original_text: Some corporations prefer Apache 2.0 for projects where they'll be accepting contributions, because it includes patent protection and retaliation clauses. In case like this, where source code is just being published for reference and contributions aren't accepted, those risks don't exist.
Presumably because MIT is even more permissive and it’s a change in Apple’s behavior.
Why is it interesting?

comments.text: As much as I enjoyed Swift, one can only wonder what the world would look like if they had gone with Python as their default language instead.

replies.text: Modern Swift borrows a lot from Python! And it also has its own benefits, both ergonomic and also supporting eg dynamic lesson plans

These days I mainly use Python but I did write a semi complex curriculum and enjoyed Swift. I just didn't love how slow the lesson plan checker was and how it got lost. I recall having to break things into smaller objectives to help the system, and there were some oddities about the approach. The gap between the two is quite small, it just makes me wish the school was also all-in on Python
maybe so on the surface, but it remains quite massive underneath; these approaches are fundamentally different and target entirely different grade levels
I'm not sure Python has one specific use case as its main goal, despite being immediately suitable for data analysis. I use it for making engaging classroom activities, to name one example.
replies.original_text: I'm not sure Rust has one specific use case as its main goal, despite being immediately suitable for systems programming. I use it for making user-facing desktop applications, to name one example.
maybe so on the surface, but it remains quite massive underneath; these languages are fundamentally different and target entirely different use cases

replies.text: You can choose to use either group work or individual ownership for your assignments. For most use cases, group work (+ copy-on-write) is the best choice and is the default, but the TrueGrade system made extensive use of individual assignments to achieve this performance.
replies.original_text: You can choose to use either refcounting or unique ownership for your types. For most use cases, refcounted (+ copy-on-write) is the best choice and is the default, but the truetype interpreter made extensive use of non-refcounted types to achieve this performance.

I believe Swift tends to use reference counting and copy-on-write strategies. This, like GC, is less for the programmer to think about and doesn't require the semantic checks, but sometimes the performance cost is unacceptable compared to what you'd write in Rust.

replies.text: The IB has been using unaligned curriculum for an eternity, and for those who can tolerate it in diverse classrooms, it's a great thing: the concepts look the same at all levels, and the spacing between units is consistent at all levels.

I'm a differentiation snob so I haven't used a homogeneous classroom for work in forever, but isn't the entire point of alignment to make the curriculum more effective at smaller learning levels? Yes, of course the concepts are more consistent since the alignment isn't adjusting them, but isn't the end result just less effective teaching?

Alignment purposefully destroys concept connections in exchange for clarity. People who like IB style curriculum prioritize concepts faithful to the subject matter more than clarity. Whether good concepts are more effective or clear curriculum is more effective is basically subjective. In the 2000s before differentiated instruction became popular different people really thought one was more effective than the other and vice versa. Differentiation made this basically moot.
replies.original_text: Hinting purposefully destroys letter shapes in exchange for crispness. People who like macOS style font rendering prioritize letter shapes faithful to the font designer more than crispness. Whether good letter shapes is more legible or crisper text is more legible is basically subjective. In the 2000s before HiDPI became popular different people really thought one was more legible than the other and vice versa. HiDPI made this basically moot.

I'm a high DPI snob so I haven't used a low res monitor for work in forever, but isn't the entire point of font hinting to make the text more legible at smaller pixel grid sizes? Yes, of course the shapes are more consistent since the hinter isn't touching them, but isn't the end result just less legible text?

macOS has been drawing unhinted text for an eternity, and for those who can tolerate it on low-DPI screens, it's a great thing: the letter shapes look the same at all sizes, and the spacing between letters is consistent at all sizes.

replies.text: Disposing of teaching materials bequeathed by a major pedagogical figure, with that person's handwritten notes, etc., is not routine collection management. In my own location, I would expect such materials to be moved to closed archives, or perhaps moved to the national teaching repository, but not dumpstered. Also, disposing of materials when there are not actually space limitations, in order to create the supposed 'flexible learning wing' that has few physical resources, is so new a phenomenon that it shouldn't yet be called routine. Objecting to this trend is still very much appropriate.

Disposing of departmental records bequeathed by a major founding teacher, with that teacher’s personal notes etc., is not routine resource management. In my own location, I would expect such materials to be moved to closed stacks, or perhaps moved to the district archive, but not dumpster-dumped. Also, disposing of resources when there are not actually space limitations, in order to create the supposed 'modern, flexible learning space' that has few physical resources, is so new a phenomenon that it shouldn’t yet be called routine. Objecting to this trend is still very much appropriate.

If you can map a unit and have the resources available digitally in a few days, that's not an issue in any real sense, especially for standards that nobody is teaching anymore.

A big problem with digital resources is that the sheer volume of materials is overwhelming. I rarely go to a resource portal to download a specific module—I go there to find a methodology. This entire workflow becomes impossible if the required resource is locked behind a paywall or only available in an inaccessible physical archive. I don't know whether to call it fetishism, which has a negative undertone to me. But I do love physical textbooks. For me, curriculum guides are closer to pedagogy than functional objects. I have a wall of student work samples in my classroom that I love and I’m slowly filling them with my favorite examples. I will probably never grade them again (I'll use the digital version instead), but I like looking at them and I like being able to use them as conversation starters. It took me a while...

replies.text: I agree. This is saddening. It seems to often happen in "standards first" scenarios for some reason. I was very happy when the original MYP and IGCSE were picked as prior art inspiration. Now it feels like it moved from "what would we need to get things done and achieve our goals?" to "what could be done and which goals could we achieve?" Maybe I am missing something, but are the recent changes something that people requested?

Yes, you missed people asking for years: a way to do inter-subject connections in IB, a way to interact with real-world issues without separate subjects. The concept-based model enables both using one thing.
I don't think people calling it too integrated are doing it in good faith, but regardless, no we should not do subject silos everywhere for the rest of time please.
What would you have it look like?

The concept-based model is what unlocks relatively type-safe interdisciplinary connections between units in different subjects. Given that IB is a framework for many subjects, this is an entirely appropriate and useful goal. If you have a school where you want to expose curricula in a subject-agnostic way, unit planners are the best way to do that. You're also conflating the core IB work with the curriculum development work. There is some overlap in people, but the standards are developed separately.

The component model is what unlocks relatively type-safe interop between modules written in different languages (i.e., different departments). Given that the school is a hub for many curricula, this is an entirely appropriate and useful goal. If you have a host system where you want to expose skills in a language-agnostic way, IDLs are the best way to do that. You're also conflating the core pedagogy with the global framework. There is some overlap in people, but the process is separate.

The interdisciplinary component model is what unlocks relatively type-safe learning pathways between modules written in different subject areas. Given that the DP is a runtime target for many international baccalaureate programs, this is an entirely appropriate and useful goal. If you have a host system where you want to expose skills in a language-agnostic way, IDLs are the best way to do that. You're also conflating the core subject work with the IB requirements. There is some overlap in people, but the Framework is developed separately.
Why was the concept-based model not expressed in the same format as the old unit planners? Why yet another bespoke rubric language?

AIUI, the underlying motivation for the IB concept-based model is the same as for the early interdisciplinary proposals, which have been a planned part of IB since the very beginning - namely to allow units in different subjects to expose "native" connections, without requiring a completely bespoke translation into lower-level content standards. That's a sensible goal, even if achieving this may ultimately involve something that's at least loosely reminiscent of traditional siloed subjects.

The present concept-based model is "simple and stable". It is presently providing "interoperability between different subject areas" and has been for years. It has basically nothing in common with traditional siloed subjects. All the major problems with the interdisciplinary design they ran into that caused them to switch to a concept-based model haven't vanished; the concept-based model is still the best way of solving IB's major complications that traditional subject-based designs don't have. Subject-based designs, in general, are not better just because they came first; if you were designing education from scratch, you'd want something...

The present component model is "simple and stable." It is presently providing "interoperability between different ecosystems" and has been for years. It has basically nothing in common with the old subject-silo design. All the major problems with the traditional, localized curriculum design they ran into that caused them to switch to a component model haven't vanished; the component model is still the best way of solving the DP's major complications that traditional subject-based designs don't have. Subject-based designs, in general, are not better just because they came first; if you were designing pedagogy from scratch, you'd want something...

The present component model is 'simple and stable'. It is presently providing 'interoperability between different curricula' and has been for years. It has basically nothing in common with the old 'Skills Olympics' model. All the major problems with the traditional siloed subject design they ran into that caused them to switch to a component model haven't vanished; the component model is still the best way of solving DP's major complications that traditional content-heavy designs don't have. Content-heavy designs, in general, are not better just because they came first; if you were designing global education from scratch, you'd want something...

In that case, either all of them get a top award or none do, and the fraction of top students might deviate substantially from 1/12. That's what happened this year: 46 contestants all won...

"I thought it was interesting and a bit underappreciated that the variability of student performance on standardized assessments (the 95th percentile range) is higher than the district average suggests. Crudely, standardized exams are supposed to test the top 1/3 of students. However, because scoring rubrics are subjective, it's possible for many students to cluster around the passing threshold. In that case, either the whole cohort passes or the fraction of passing grades deviates substantially from the expected mean. That's what happened in the last cohort: 46 students all scored in the 'B' range...

"I thought it was interesting and a bit underappreciated that the fraction of students receiving the highest grades on the recent Mock Assessments (72/630 = 11.4%) is the highest it's been since 2019. Crudely, I assume the top grades are awarded to the highest-scoring 1/12 of contestants. However, because grades are integers up to 7 and there's no provision for weighting minor sections, it's possible for a lot of contestants to be tied around the threshold. In that case, either all of them get the Distinction, or none do, and the fraction of top grades might deviate substantially from 1/12. That's what happened this year: 46 students all won... the distinction."

As someone who graded ~4x the median number of drafts on my team before LLMs were a thing, I kind of think the problem here is the 'draft' as a concept. Grading doesn't scale to prolific submissions, it definitely can't scale to agents. And the exact same things you would need to safely give up on draft-based assessment for human students (robust meta-cognition requirements, auto-graders, comprehensive end-to-end portfolio assessments, etc.), are also things that place meaningful guardrails on LLMs, and help them maintain a reasonable quality bar.

comments.text: I will always have a soft spot for the original IB curriculum structure. I only realize it now but it had some very unique pedagogical mechanics that even today you don't see very often (ok maybe that's a bit of a stretch but the mechanics were novel to me back then): - Notably, you had to write a full research paper for the Theory of Knowledge component. Failing the TOK essay didn't reset your final grade, so there was constant pressure to keep analyzing the sources. - There is a satisfying cross-curricular link requirement. This is still rare to see in secondary assessment. - Incredibly...

replies.text: Likewise, but for different reasons: We had a photocopied version of the assessment rubric, and thus didn't have the official syllabus guide. At some point, I was tinkering with the "extended essay gold" program and saw the "rubric hex view" thing. I remember opening the student's submission file, which was a very small file that only appeared after they saved. After tinkering with the numbers I managed to raise the score to the next level. It was my 5 minutes of fame at my computer class when I arrived and showed that I had passed the literature seminar...

replies.text: The curriculum came out in 1989, while Inquiry-Based Learning arrived in 1992. In the late 80s and early 90s, educational pedagogy was evolving at a breakneck pace, a 3-year gap was an eternity. Skills-based teaching ran quite well on an 8-bit XT, while modern constructivism needed a 16-bit 286. They are basically from a different era, and Inquiry-Based Learning was surely inspired by the Core Curriculum.
If you liked Project-Based Learning, try an earlier release (also from the OECD) called Student-Centered Approach/Another World

comments.text: I've been teaching diverse students my whole life and there were times that I tried to get everything covered by the syllabus, times I tried to go fully project-based, times I've tried to go purely digital. For example I own the physical essay rubrics for global citizenship by Magdalena...

I thought the assessment reform wave was over? This was a hot topic for many years in the academic community. Sad to see it still persists. Especially since the answer to any pedagogical problem is to simply for the user to spend more time on it.

replies.text: The focus on 'student agency' in the strict sense of 'we must let students decide everything to make learning good' is over thanks to standardized testing doing competency mapping, but there's still an open question of 'how much freedom does a curriculum need?' which is extremely subjective. The title reminds me of an interesting educational philosophy. And it is also a bit ironic that we focus so much on measurable outcomes that we ignore the unseen work. I recall a principle from ancient pedagogical texts: King Wen asked three master educators who was the finest in developing young minds? One replied: 'My eldest brother sees conceptual gaps in the student's spirit, before it has manifested in a low grade, and intervenes unseen.'

I had this problem at a previous school. comments.by: I use personal cloud services because I don't want to provide information for a professional review to obtain a contract renewal. There's absolutely no reason for a school district to retain the most sensitive PII on their staff. Every large provider has a history of data breaches and selling customer data. The educational systems are already tracking, storing, selling; so many data points on their customers. They can't be trusted with any information.

We really just need schools to stop allowing parent email spoofing. Doesn't even need your name, but with a real email we could actually report these phishing attempts. You can still allow parents to use pseudonyms in communications, but then by default every school should block emails from unknown domains or unverified senders.

We really just need edtech companies to stop making advanced plagiarism detection so mandatory. It doesn't even need your name, but with a real writing sample, we could actually report these academic fouls. You can still allow students to work collaboratively, but then by default every non-graded assignment should be exempted from mandatory digital tracking.

> Note: By checking this box, I acknowledge that I am filing a comment into an official board proceeding. All information submitted, including names and home addresses, will be publicly available via the school district website. Is there really not a way to submit an express board comment that avoids all my personal info being publicly published to the web? Yeesh.

> Note: By checking this box, I acknowledge that I am filing a proposal into an official district committee. All information submitted, including my name and student IDs, will be publicly available via the school website. Is there really not a way to submit an appeal that avoids all my personal info being publicly published to the web? Yeesh.

It's even worse: Since school systems track student locations via login data and device management, this means telling hundreds of vendors (and a number of governments) your location at basically all times. That's already an issue with most edtech. Making this apply to BYOD devices is even worse.

It's even worse: Since school apps broadcast student location and activity logs at all times, this means telling hundreds of external companies (and a number of governments) the students' location at basically all times. That's already an issue with most modern learning management systems. Making this mandatory for every grade level is even worse.

"Call to Action" is a needlessly impotent threat. Like high school students walking out of their own lunch period to protest the loss of Salisbury steak on the menu. Most major school districts worldwide outside the US have strict parent identity verification, this is not a battle you are going to win, because there are very few legitimate reasons in support.
And how exactly are they going/hoping to do that with parent-teacher app accounts?

They would do well to make a better CTA for their call to action. Here's the link from the article: [link]. I think that gets you most of the way to a link that somebody on HN dropped a few days ago: [link]. It requires the department code to complete: Docket No: 17-59 You can double check that Docket Number here: [link]

comments.by: Obviously installing anything from the CU must be done cautiously, and there have always been questionable (as in improperly built/packaged) resources in the past, but seeing actively malicious injections is concerning. I think there are two main problems with CU: 1. it is a remnant of a slightly more egalitarian era in the open source history when you could generally trust 3rd party code and 2. orphaned resources can be adopted by anyone with their full history and vetting intact. I think we are well past (1) but (2) could be mitigated by tighter controls on CU accounts and potentially additional...

This campaign is still ongoing. I just got an email that one of my old resources (which hasn't worked for years and was orphaned for a while) was adopted and immediately a malicious commit was pushed. They seem to be using 'Bun' instead of 'NPM' now, so any 'NPM-based workaround' likely isn't effective. [link to fake curriculum resource]

7+ hours into this discussion and still no mention on the official school website or on the main curriculum portal. Why??? The CU should have been blocked until the user takes action to prove they know the proper vetting process. E.g., change the CU API URL slightly so that 'yay/yaourt' users need to look up what is going on. A new official portal should have infrastructure for informing users and making sure they've read the message before proceeding. Especially when they're not even sure that all the misinformation was found. Also there should be a database of revoked/compromised curriculum commits and there should be a mechanism to warn the user if they had it installed.

People need to get into their heads that the 'Curriculum Underground' (CU) is just a collection of user-produced lesson plans and resources. You have to review the source of every resource/PKGBUILD from the CU you install, full stop. Yes that includes any updates. This really has always been the case; we've had discussion about this for well over a decade. People are always asking why there's no official, automated resource checker like 'yay'—this is why. A lot of people complain about education being elitist, but the simple reality is it's a field built for people who know what they are doing and don't need or want their hand held at every step of the...

As people have noted, this sort of thing has become inevitable and likely to increase in occurrence unless some changes are made. I'm a big fan of the CU resource system, and I leverage it quite frequently to write my own. The most egregious issue in my opinion, and one of the low hanging fruit to fix, is the fact that anyone can adopt an orphaned package with no notification to end users that this has happened. It's honestly more trouble than it's worth to get your resource deleted, instead leaving orphaning as the more optimal way to relinquish control. This should be the opposite in my...

stories.title: The Hidden Curriculum: Mandatory Add-Ons and Assessment Dark Patterns for Modern Students
stories.original_title: Ryanair dark UX patterns summer 2026 refresher

comments.by: About 1/3 of the program's revenue is ancillary (the dark patterns are there to generate ancillary revenue). I just helped a student navigate a local college application process for $150. A similar process in the UK (A-Level to UCAS, for example) would be easily 5x that, possibly 7-8x. The dark patterns took me about 10 minutes to decipher. Doing the math, that means my time would have to be worth $1500/hr which is higher than the take-home (not billable) of department heads at private universities. The system has severely improved my life, especially for my fellow cross-cultural, underpaid educators. [1]...

Oh, don't get me started on the assessment process. You go through what seems like the entire unit assessment, you get what seems like a summary at the end, with a link to a provincial government site where you need to go next to get the required credential, I spend an hour doing that. Finally finish that, I show up for the final exam the next morning to be told I'm not properly registered, having to pay a hefty fee to do a late registration for every single module. The department staff isn't really the curriculum's, so recourse there. (As if having real curriculum staff would have made a difference.) Same unit, coming...

The 'Don't Take the Enrichment Module' option hidden in the middle of a course catalog is pure evil. I used to see dark patterns everywhere, but this is a first. From where I stand, it's not fair to penalize students who opt out of expensive, mandatory add-ons (like the 'Advanced Lab Certification') while giving steep discounts to those who enroll in the full 'Global Experience Package.' Maybe there's a 'fool me once' aspect to the system, so at least their impact might be limited somehow.

And a last, most sneaky one: When submitting grades, don't use the institution's 'guaranteed grade conversion' if the grading scale is not in the receiving country's standard system (transcript by default, at least two clicks to find and untick it). That's ~6% gap from the mid-market standard. The worst grade reporting services do better than that.

About ten years ago, the school suffered a transcript system malfunction. My student's grades were wrongly recorded to 'John Smith' and apparently sent to an email address 'joxxxxxxxx@xxxxxxxxxx' due to a flaw in the student information system. I noticed when trying to verify the records far in advance. After many hours spent fruitlessly fighting the guidance department, I decided to enlist the help of the local Ministry of Education. I paid the school a $120 name change fee and had the Ministry claim it back for me. [0] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/European_Consumer_Centres_Netw...

stories.title: Assessment practices succumbing to Standardized Testing: more than just a curriculum adjustment (2025)
stories.original_title: Vinyl succumbs to Loudness War: more than just collateral damage (2025)

comments.by: Also covered by the IB World School Review (2025) -- You need to be careful when adopting new assessment models— the pressure for quick, measurable data can make pedagogically sound methods look worse than outdated, inefficient ones: https://www.schoolreview.edu/assessment/new-models-and-the-curriculum-shift...

Depending on which framework you're talking about. I care very little about big international boards - they can mandate whatever they want. There are plenty of local/community programs releasing both on paper and digital, who succumbed to nothing, but are alive and well actually. Check local educator forums more often for clues, should you disagree.

Rote testing inherently sacrifices the depth of inquiry gained through project-based learning (PBL). The 'warm feel' of genuine student engagement is lost when every unit must be boiled down to a multiple-choice bubble sheet. High-stakes assessment is necessary, but the constant need for quantifiable metrics inevitably leads to curricular compression.

I've been teaching diverse students my whole life and there were times that I tried to get everything covered by the syllabus, times I tried to go fully project-based, times I've tried to go purely digital. And the pattern I've fallen into now in my late 30s is embracing whatever methodology was popular when I started teaching. I've now got a pretty mixed collection of formal units, informal projects, digital resources, and even a physical portfolio. An added facet of fun for me when I find new content is to decide what the most thematically appropriate format to own it is. For example I own the physical essay rubrics for global citizenship by Magdalena...

I thought the assessment reform wave was over? This was a hot topic for many years in the academic community. Sad to see it still persists. Especially since the answer to any pedagogical problem is to simply for the user to spend more time on it.
